=== PLAN ===
Build a p-code VM and p-code assembler (pasm) for the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA. The VM is a stack-based interpreter written in COR24 assembly. The pasm tool (also COR24 assembly) assembles human-readable .pasm source into bytecode for testing the VM. Pascal compiler is a separate project.

=== STEPS ===
  [ ] 001-vm-scaffold [production]: Create pvm.s scaffold: COR24 entry point, VM state struct (pc, esp, csp, fp, ... <-- YOU ARE HERE
  [ ] 002-vm-stack-ops [legacy]: Implement push/push_s/dup/drop/swap/over opcode handlers and sys PUTC (opcode 0x60 with id=1). Test by embedding hardcoded bytecode: push_s 72, sys 1, push_s 10, sys 1, halt — should print H-newline to UART.
  [ ] 003-vm-arithmetic [legacy]: Implement add/sub/mul/div/mod/neg/and/or/xor/not/shl/shr and eq/ne/lt/le/gt/ge opcode handlers. Test with hardcoded bytecode: push 7, push 6, mul, sys PUTC should output ASCII '*' (42).
  [ ] 004-vm-control-flow [legacy]: Implement jmp/jz/jnz opcode handlers. Test with hardcoded countdown loop: push 5, loop: dup, jz done, push_s 1, sub, jmp loop, done: halt.
  [ ] 005-vm-frames [legacy]: Implement call/ret/enter/leave and loadl/storel/loada/storea opcode handlers. Build frame management: push/pop activation records on call stack, argument passing from eval stack to frame. Test with hardcoded bytecode for a factorial function.
  [ ] 006-vm-memory [legacy]: Implement loadg/storeg/addrg/addrl and indirect load/store/loadb/storeb. Implement globals segment. Test with hardcoded bytecode: global counter, store/load/print.
  [ ] 007-vm-nonlocal [legacy]: Implement loadn/storen for static link chain traversal (needed for Pascal nested procedures). Test with hardcoded 2-level nested procedure accessing outer local.
  [ ] 008-vm-syscalls [legacy]: Implement remaining sys calls: HALT(0), GETC(2), LED(3), ALLOC(4), FREE(5). Bump allocator for heap. Test: LED toggle, UART echo, heap alloc/write/read.
  [ ] 009-vm-traps [legacy]: Implement trap handling for all trap codes (div-zero, stack overflow/underflow, invalid opcode, invalid address, nil pointer). Print diagnostic to UART on trap. Test each trap condition.
  [ ] 010-pasm-lexer [legacy]: Create pasm.s — p-code assembler in COR24 assembly. Start with lexer: read pasm source from UART, tokenize into token types (mnemonic, number, label, directive, newline, comment, eof). Use a token buffer in memory. Test by feeding simple pasm source and dumping token stream.
  [ ] 011-pasm-parser [legacy]: Implement pasm parser: two-pass assembly. Pass 1: collect labels and procedure addresses, count globals/data sizes. Pass 2: emit bytecode. Handle .const/.global/.data/.proc/.end directives. Test by assembling a minimal program (push 42, halt) and verifying bytecode bytes.
  [ ] 012-pasm-integration [legacy]: Connect pasm output to VM input. Assemble pasm source into bytecode buffer, then execute. Test with hello.pasm (full puts procedure, data string, main calling puts). First end-to-end test.
  [ ] 013-test-suite [legacy]: Create full test suite: tests/t01-arith.pasm through t10-traps.pasm. Create demo.sh test harness with golden output comparison (following tf24a pattern). Create examples/hello.pasm, examples/blink.pasm, examples/echo.pasm.
  [ ] 014-hardening [legacy]: Fix bugs found by test suite. Add stack depth checking. Validate memory bounds. Clean up diagnostic output. Ensure all tests pass. Final documentation update.

=== CURRENT STEP: 001-vm-scaffold [production] ===
Status: pending
Description: Create pvm.s scaffold: COR24 entry point, VM state struct (pc, esp, csp, fp, ...
Task type: cor24-asm

=== PROMPT ===
Create pvm.s scaffold: COR24 entry point, VM state struct (pc, esp, csp, fp, gp, hp, code, status, trap_code), memory segment initialization (code/globals/call-stack/eval-stack/heap base addresses and limits), and the main fetch-decode-execute loop skeleton (fetch opcode byte from code[pc], pc++, dispatch via jump table to opcode handlers — stub all handlers as halt-with-unknown-opcode for now). Include UART output helper (tx_wait polling loop at 0xFF0100/0xFF0101) for diagnostic printing. Test: cor24-run --run pvm.s --speed 0 should assemble clean and halt immediately. Reference tf24a/forth.s for COR24 idioms (label-on-own-line, .byte/.word, register constraints, UART polling pattern).

=== WHEN DONE ===
Run: agentrail begin    (if not yet started)
Run: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "next instructions"
  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

1→# pv24a — Design Document
     2→
     3→## 1. VM State
     4→
     5→The p-code VM maintains the following state in a memory struct:
     6→
     7→```
     8→vm_state:
     9→    .word 0     ; pc   — p-code instruction pointer
    10→    .word 0     ; esp  — eval stack pointer (top of eval stack)
    11→    .word 0     ; csp  — call stack pointer (top of call stack)
    12→    .word 0     ; fp   — current frame pointer (call stack)
    13→    .word 0     ; gp   — globals base address
    14→    .word 0     ; hp   — heap pointer (next free)
    15→    .word 0     ; code — base address of p-code segment
    16→    .word 0     ; status — 0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped
    17→    .word 0     ; trap_code — last trap reason
    18→```
    19→
    20→## 2. P-Code Instruction Set
    21→
    22→### 2.1 Stack / Constants
    23→
    24→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    25→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    26→| `push` imm24 | 4 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push 24-bit immediate |
    27→| `push_s` imm8 | 2 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push sign-extended 8-bit immediate |
    28→| `dup` | 1 byte | ( a -- a a ) | Duplicate top |
    29→| `drop` | 1 byte | ( a -- ) | Discard top |
    30→| `swap` | 1 byte | ( a b -- b a ) | Swap top two |
    31→| `over` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a b a ) | Copy second to top |
    32→
    33→### 2.2 Arithmetic / Logic
    34→
    35→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    36→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    37→| `add` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a+b ) | Add |
    38→| `sub` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a-b ) | Subtract |
    39→| `mul` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a*b ) | Multiply |
    40→| `div` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a/b ) | Divide (trap on b=0) |
    41→| `mod` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a%b ) | Modulo (trap on b=0) |
    42→| `neg` | 1 byte | ( a -- -a ) | Negate |
    43→| `and` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a&b ) | Bitwise AND |
    44→| `or` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a\|b ) | Bitwise OR |
    45→| `xor` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a^b ) | Bitwise XOR |
    46→| `not` | 1 byte | ( a -- ~a ) | Bitwise NOT |
    47→| `shl` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a<<n ) | Shift left |
    48→| `shr` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a>>n ) | Arithmetic shift right |
    49→
    50→### 2.3 Comparison
    51→
    52→All comparisons push 1 (true) or 0 (false):
    53→
    54→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    55→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    56→| `eq` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Equal |
    57→| `ne` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Not equal |
    58→| `lt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less than (signed) |
    59→| `le` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less or equal (signed) |
    60→| `gt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater than (signed) |
    61→| `ge` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater or equal (signed) |
    62→
    63→### 2.4 Control Flow
    64→
    65→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    66→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    67→| `jmp` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( -- ) | Unconditional jump |
    68→| `jz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if zero |
    69→| `jnz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if nonzero |
    70→| `call` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call procedure |
    71→| `ret` nargs8 | 2 bytes | ( [retval] -- [retval] ) | Return, clean nargs |
    72→| `halt` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Stop VM |
    73→| `trap` code8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Trigger trap |
    74→
    75→### 2.5 Local / Global / Nonlocal Access
    76→
    77→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    78→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    79→| `enter` nlocals8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Set up frame, reserve N local slots |
    80→| `leave` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Tear down frame |
    81→| `loadl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load local at offset |
    82→| `storel` off8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store local at offset |
    83→| `loadg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load global at offset |
    84→| `storeg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store global at offset |
    85→| `addrl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of local |
    86→| `addrg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of global |
    87→| `loadn` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load nonlocal via static link chain |
    88→| `storen` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store nonlocal via static link chain |
    89→
    90→### 2.6 Indirect Memory Access
    91→
    92→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    93→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    94→| `load` | 1 byte | ( addr -- val ) | Load word from address |
    95→| `store` | 1 byte | ( val addr -- ) | Store word to address |
    96→| `loadb` | 1 byte | ( addr -- byte ) | Load byte (zero-extended) |
    97→| `storeb` | 1 byte | ( byte addr -- ) | Store byte |
    98→
    99→### 2.7 System / I/O
   100→
   101→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
   102→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
   103→| `sys` id8 | 2 bytes | (varies) | System call |
   104→
   105→System call IDs:
   106→
   107→| ID | Name | Stack Effect | Description |
   108→|----|------|-------------|-------------|
   109→| 0 | HALT | ( -- ) | Stop execution |
   110→| 1 | PUTC | ( char -- ) | Write byte to UART |
   111→| 2 | GETC | ( -- char ) | Read byte from UART (blocking) |
   112→| 3 | LED | ( state -- ) | Write LED state |
   113→| 4 | ALLOC | ( size -- ptr ) | Bump-allocate heap block |
   114→| 5 | FREE | ( ptr -- ) | Free heap block (no-op in bump mode) |
   115→
   116→### 2.8 Argument Access
   117→
   118→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
   119→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
   120→| `loada` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load argument by index |
   121→| `storea` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store argument by index |
   122→
   123→## 3. Call Frame Layout
   124→
   125→When `call` executes, a new frame is pushed onto the call stack:
   126→
   127→```
   128→         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
   129→         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
   130→         │ ...              │
   131→         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
   132→fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
   133→         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
   134→         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (lexically enclosing frame)
   135→         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
   136→         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
   137→         ├──────────────────┤
   138→         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
   139→         │ ...              │
   140→         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
   141→         └──────────────────┘
   142→```
   143→
   144→- **Caller** pushes arguments onto eval stack, then `call addr`
   145→- **call** instruction: pops args from eval stack into call stack frame header,
   146→  pushes return PC, dynamic link, static link, proc ID
   147→- **enter N**: advances csp by N*3 to reserve local slots, sets fp
   148→- **leave**: restores fp to dynamic link, restores csp
   149→- **ret N**: pushes return value (if any) to eval stack, pops frame, cleans N args
   150→
   151→## 4. Calling Convention
   152→
   153→Simple and explicit:
   154→
   155→1. Caller pushes arguments left-to-right onto eval stack
   156→2. Caller executes `call <proc_addr>`
   157→3. Callee executes `enter <nlocals>` (reserves local slots)
   158→4. Callee accesses args via `loada 0`, `loada 1`, etc.
   159→5. Callee accesses locals via `loadl 0`, `loadl 1`, etc.
   160→6. For functions: callee pushes return value onto eval stack
   161→7. Callee executes `ret <nargs>` (cleans args from frame)
   162→8. Caller finds return value (if any) on eval stack
   163→
   164→## 5. P-Code Assembler (pasm) Syntax
   165→
   166→### 5.1 Source Format
   167→
   168→```
   169→; Comment (semicolon to end of line)
   170→
   171→.const UART_DATA  -65280      ; named constant
   172→.const UART_STATUS -65279
   173→.const LED_PORT   -65024
   174→
   175→.global counter 1             ; 1 word of global storage
   176→.global buffer 32             ; 32 words
   177→
   178→.data msg 72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 0   ; "Hello\0" as bytes
   179→
   180→.proc main 0                 ; procedure, 0 locals
   181→    push msg                  ; push address of msg
   182→    call puts
   183→    halt
   184→.end
   185→
   186→.proc puts 1                 ; 1 local (loop pointer)
   187→    loada 0                   ; arg0 = string address
   188→    storel 0                  ; local0 = current ptr
   189→loop:
   190→    loadl 0
   191→    loadb                     ; load byte at ptr
   192→    dup
   193→    jz done                   ; if zero, done
   194→    sys 1                     ; PUTC
   195→    loadl 0
   196→    push 1
   197→    add
   198→    storel 0                  ; ptr++
   199→    jmp loop
   200→done:
   201→    drop                      ; drop the zero
   202→    ret 1                     ; return, clean 1 arg
   203→.end
   204→```
   205→
   206→### 5.2 Directives
   207→
   208→| Directive | Syntax | Description |
   209→|-----------|--------|-------------|
   210→| `.const` | `.const NAME value` | Define named constant |
   211→| `.global` | `.global NAME nwords` | Reserve global storage |
   212→| `.data` | `.data NAME byte, byte, ...` | Define byte data (string/array) |
   213→| `.proc` | `.proc NAME nlocals` | Begin procedure (nlocals = local slot count) |
   214→| `.end` | `.end` | End procedure |
   215→
   216→### 5.3 Labels
   217→
   218→Labels appear on their own line, ending with `:`:
   219→
   220→```
   221→loop:
   222→    ; instructions...
   223→    jmp loop
   224→```
   225→
   226→Labels are local to the enclosing `.proc` / `.end` block.
   227→
   228→### 5.4 Integer Literals
   229→
   230→- Decimal: `42`, `-1`, `0`
   231→- Hex: `0xFF` (if supported) or decimal equivalents
   232→- Named constants: `UART_DATA` (substituted by assembler)
   233→
   234→### 5.5 Instructions
   235→
   236→One instruction per line. Operand follows mnemonic separated by whitespace:
   237→
   238→```
   239→push 42
   240→loadl 0
   241→jz done
   242→call puts
   243→sys 1
   244→ret 0
   245→```
   246→
   247→## 6. Opcode Encoding Table
   248→
   249→Opcodes are assigned sequential byte values:
   250→
   251→| Byte | Mnemonic | Operand |
   252→|------|----------|---------|
   253→| 0x00 | halt | — |
   254→| 0x01 | push | imm24 |
   255→| 0x02 | push_s | imm8 |
   256→| 0x03 | dup | — |
   257→| 0x04 | drop | — |
   258→| 0x05 | swap | — |
   259→| 0x06 | over | — |
   260→| 0x10 | add | — |
   261→| 0x11 | sub | — |
   262→| 0x12 | mul | — |
   263→| 0x13 | div | — |
   264→| 0x14 | mod | — |
   265→| 0x15 | neg | — |
   266→| 0x16 | and | — |
   267→| 0x17 | or | — |
   268→| 0x18 | xor | — |
   269→| 0x19 | not | — |
   270→| 0x1A | shl | — |
   271→| 0x1B | shr | — |
   272→| 0x20 | eq | — |
   273→| 0x21 | ne | — |
   274→| 0x22 | lt | — |
   275→| 0x23 | le | — |
   276→| 0x24 | gt | — |
   277→| 0x25 | ge | — |
   278→| 0x30 | jmp | addr24 |
   279→| 0x31 | jz | addr24 |
   280→| 0x32 | jnz | addr24 |
   281→| 0x33 | call | addr24 |
   282→| 0x34 | ret | nargs8 |
   283→| 0x35 | trap | code8 |
   284→| 0x40 | enter | nlocals8 |
   285→| 0x41 | leave | — |
   286→| 0x42 | loadl | off8 |
   287→| 0x43 | storel | off8 |
   288→| 0x44 | loadg | off24 |
   289→| 0x45 | storeg | off24 |
   290→| 0x46 | addrl | off8 |
   291→| 0x47 | addrg | off24 |
   292→| 0x48 | loada | idx8 |
   293→| 0x49 | storea | idx8 |
   294→| 0x4A | loadn | depth8 off8 |
   295→| 0x4B | storen | depth8 off8 |
   296→| 0x50 | load | — |
   297→| 0x51 | store | — |
   298→| 0x52 | loadb | — |
   299→| 0x53 | storeb | — |
   300→| 0x60 | sys | id8 |
   301→
   302→Reserved ranges 0x70–0xFF for future opcodes (heap, debug, extended ops).
   303→
   304→## 7. Trap Codes
   305→
   306→| Code | Name | Trigger |
   307→|------|------|---------|
   308→| 0 | USER_TRAP | `trap 0` instruction |
   309→| 1 | DIV_ZERO | Division/modulo by zero |
   310→| 2 | STACK_OVERFLOW | Eval or call stack exceeds limit |
   311→| 3 | STACK_UNDERFLOW | Eval stack pop when empty |
   312→| 4 | INVALID_OPCODE | Unknown opcode byte |
   313→| 5 | INVALID_ADDRESS | Memory access out of bounds |
   314→| 6 | NIL_POINTER | Dereference of address 0 |
   315→| 7 | BOUNDS_CHECK | Array index out of bounds |
   316→
   317→On trap: VM sets status=2, trap_code=N, halts. Diagnostic message to UART.
   318→
   319→## 8. Memory Segment Layout
   320→
   321→At VM initialization:
   322→
   323→1. **Code segment** starts at a fixed base address (after VM code)
   324→2. **Constants/data** follows code (emitted by pasm)
   325→3. **Globals** follow data (allocated per `.global` directives)
   326→4. **Call stack** starts after globals, grows upward
   327→5. **Eval stack** starts after call stack region, grows upward
   328→6. **Heap** starts after eval stack region, grows upward
   329→7. Segment base addresses stored in VM state struct
   330→
   331→Sizes are configurable at VM init time. Default allocations:
   332→- Call stack: 4 KB
   333→- Eval stack: 2 KB
   334→- Heap: remaining SRAM up to ~900 KB
   335→
   336→## 9. Implementation Strategy
   337→
   338→### Phase 1: Minimal VM
   339→- Opcode dispatch loop
   340→- push, dup, drop, add, sub, halt
   341→- UART output via sys PUTC
   342→- Hardcoded test bytecode (no pasm yet)
   343→
   344→### Phase 2: Control Flow + Procedures
   345→- jmp, jz, jnz
   346→- call, ret, enter, leave
   347→- loadl, storel, loada, storea
   348→- Frame management
   349→
   350→### Phase 3: Full Instruction Set
   351→- All arithmetic/logic/comparison opcodes
   352→- Globals (loadg, storeg)
   353→- Indirect memory (load, store, loadb, storeb)
   354→- Nonlocal access (loadn, storen)
   355→- All sys calls
   356→

# pv24a — Design Document

## 1. VM State

The p-code VM maintains the following state in a memory struct:

```
vm_state:
    .word 0     ; pc   — p-code instruction pointer
    .word 0     ; esp  — eval stack pointer (top of eval stack)
    .word 0     ; csp  — call stack pointer (top of call stack)
    .word 0     ; fp   — current frame pointer (call stack)
    .word 0     ; gp   — globals base address
    .word 0     ; hp   — heap pointer (next free)
    .word 0     ; code — base address of p-code segment
    .word 0     ; status — 0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped
    .word 0     ; trap_code — last trap reason
```

## 2. P-Code Instruction Set

### 2.1 Stack / Constants

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `push` imm24 | 4 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push 24-bit immediate |
| `push_s` imm8 | 2 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push sign-extended 8-bit immediate |
| `dup` | 1 byte | ( a -- a a ) | Duplicate top |
| `drop` | 1 byte | ( a -- ) | Discard top |
| `swap` | 1 byte | ( a b -- b a ) | Swap top two |
| `over` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a b a ) | Copy second to top |

### 2.2 Arithmetic / Logic

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `add` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a+b ) | Add |
| `sub` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a-b ) | Subtract |
| `mul` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a*b ) | Multiply |
| `div` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a/b ) | Divide (trap on b=0) |
| `mod` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a%b ) | Modulo (trap on b=0) |
| `neg` | 1 byte | ( a -- -a ) | Negate |
| `and` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a&b ) | Bitwise AND |
| `or` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a\|b ) | Bitwise OR |
| `xor` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a^b ) | Bitwise XOR |
| `not` | 1 byte | ( a -- ~a ) | Bitwise NOT |
| `shl` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a<<n ) | Shift left |
| `shr` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a>>n ) | Arithmetic shift right |

### 2.3 Comparison

All comparisons push 1 (true) or 0 (false):

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `eq` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Equal |
| `ne` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Not equal |
| `lt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less than (signed) |
| `le` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less or equal (signed) |
| `gt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater than (signed) |
| `ge` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater or equal (signed) |

### 2.4 Control Flow

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `jmp` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( -- ) | Unconditional jump |
| `jz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if zero |
| `jnz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if nonzero |
| `call` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call procedure |
| `ret` nargs8 | 2 bytes | ( [retval] -- [retval] ) | Return, clean nargs |
| `halt` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Stop VM |
| `trap` code8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Trigger trap |

### 2.5 Local / Global / Nonlocal Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `enter` nlocals8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Set up frame, reserve N local slots |
| `leave` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Tear down frame |
| `loadl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load local at offset |
| `storel` off8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store local at offset |
| `loadg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load global at offset |
| `storeg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store global at offset |
| `addrl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of local |
| `addrg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of global |
| `loadn` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load nonlocal via static link chain |
| `storen` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store nonlocal via static link chain |

### 2.6 Indirect Memory Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `load` | 1 byte | ( addr -- val ) | Load word from address |
| `store` | 1 byte | ( val addr -- ) | Store word to address |
| `loadb` | 1 byte | ( addr -- byte ) | Load byte (zero-extended) |
| `storeb` | 1 byte | ( byte addr -- ) | Store byte |

### 2.7 System / I/O

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `sys` id8 | 2 bytes | (varies) | System call |

System call IDs:

| ID | Name | Stack Effect | Description |
|----|------|-------------|-------------|
| 0 | HALT | ( -- ) | Stop execution |
| 1 | PUTC | ( char -- ) | Write byte to UART |
| 2 | GETC | ( -- char ) | Read byte from UART (blocking) |
| 3 | LED | ( state -- ) | Write LED state |
| 4 | ALLOC | ( size -- ptr ) | Bump-allocate heap block |
| 5 | FREE | ( ptr -- ) | Free heap block (no-op in bump mode) |

### 2.8 Argument Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `loada` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load argument by index |
| `storea` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store argument by index |

## 3. Call Frame Layout

When `call` executes, a new frame is pushed onto the call stack:

```
         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
         │ ...              │
         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (lexically enclosing frame)
         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
         ├──────────────────┤
         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
         │ ...              │
         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
         └──────────────────┘
```

- **Caller** pushes arguments onto eval stack, then `call addr`
- **call** instruction: pops args from eval stack into call stack frame header,
  pushes return PC, dynamic link, static link, proc ID
- **enter N**: advances csp by N*3 to reserve local slots, sets fp
- **leave**: restores fp to dynamic link, restores csp
- **ret N**: pushes return value (if any) to eval stack, pops frame, cleans N args

## 4. Calling Convention

Simple and explicit:

1. Caller pushes arguments left-to-right onto eval stack
2. Caller executes `call <proc_addr>`
3. Callee executes `enter <nlocals>` (reserves local slots)
4. Callee accesses args via `loada 0`, `loada 1`, etc.
5. Callee accesses locals via `loadl 0`, `loadl 1`, etc.
6. For functions: callee pushes return value onto eval stack
7. Callee executes `ret <nargs>` (cleans args from frame)
8. Caller finds return value (if any) on eval stack

## 5. P-Code Assembler (pasm) Syntax

### 5.1 Source Format

```
; Comment (semicolon to end of line)

.const UART_DATA  -65280      ; named constant
.const UART_STATUS -65279
.const LED_PORT   -65024

.global counter 1             ; 1 word of global storage
.global buffer 32             ; 32 words

.data msg 72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 0   ; "Hello\0" as bytes

.proc main 0                 ; procedure, 0 locals
    push msg                  ; push address of msg
    call puts
    halt
.end

.proc puts 1                 ; 1 local (loop pointer)
    loada 0                   ; arg0 = string address
    storel 0                  ; local0 = current ptr
loop:
    loadl 0
    loadb                     ; load byte at ptr
    dup
    jz done                   ; if zero, done
    sys 1                     ; PUTC
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                  ; ptr++
    jmp loop
done:
    drop                      ; drop the zero
    ret 1                     ; return, clean 1 arg
.end
```

### 5.2 Directives

| Directive | Syntax | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| `.const` | `.const NAME value` | Define named constant |
| `.global` | `.global NAME nwords` | Reserve global storage |
| `.data` | `.data NAME byte, byte, ...` | Define byte data (string/array) |
| `.proc` | `.proc NAME nlocals` | Begin procedure (nlocals = local slot count) |
| `.end` | `.end` | End procedure |

### 5.3 Labels

Labels appear on their own line, ending with `:`:

```
loop:
    ; instructions...
    jmp loop
```

Labels are local to the enclosing `.proc` / `.end` block.

### 5.4 Integer Literals

- Decimal: `42`, `-1`, `0`
- Hex: `0xFF` (if supported) or decimal equivalents
- Named constants: `UART_DATA` (substituted by assembler)

### 5.5 Instructions

One instruction per line. Operand follows mnemonic separated by whitespace:

```
push 42
loadl 0
jz done
call puts
sys 1
ret 0
```

## 6. Opcode Encoding Table

Opcodes are assigned sequential byte values:

| Byte | Mnemonic | Operand |
|------|----------|---------|
| 0x00 | halt | — |
| 0x01 | push | imm24 |
| 0x02 | push_s | imm8 |
| 0x03 | dup | — |
| 0x04 | drop | — |
| 0x05 | swap | — |
| 0x06 | over | — |
| 0x10 | add | — |
| 0x11 | sub | — |
| 0x12 | mul | — |
| 0x13 | div | — |
| 0x14 | mod | — |
| 0x15 | neg | — |
| 0x16 | and | — |
| 0x17 | or | — |
| 0x18 | xor | — |
| 0x19 | not | — |
| 0x1A | shl | — |
| 0x1B | shr | — |
| 0x20 | eq | — |
| 0x21 | ne | — |
| 0x22 | lt | — |
| 0x23 | le | — |
| 0x24 | gt | — |
| 0x25 | ge | — |
| 0x30 | jmp | addr24 |
| 0x31 | jz | addr24 |
| 0x32 | jnz | addr24 |
| 0x33 | call | addr24 |
| 0x34 | ret | nargs8 |
| 0x35 | trap | code8 |
| 0x40 | enter | nlocals8 |
| 0x41 | leave | — |
| 0x42 | loadl | off8 |
| 0x43 | storel | off8 |
| 0x44 | loadg | off24 |
| 0x45 | storeg | off24 |
| 0x46 | addrl | off8 |
| 0x47 | addrg | off24 |
| 0x48 | loada | idx8 |
| 0x49 | storea | idx8 |
| 0x4A | loadn | depth8 off8 |
| 0x4B | storen | depth8 off8 |
| 0x50 | load | — |
| 0x51 | store | — |
| 0x52 | loadb | — |
| 0x53 | storeb | — |
| 0x60 | sys | id8 |

Reserved ranges 0x70–0xFF for future opcodes (heap, debug, extended ops).

## 7. Trap Codes

| Code | Name | Trigger |
|------|------|---------|
| 0 | USER_TRAP | `trap 0` instruction |
| 1 | DIV_ZERO | Division/modulo by zero |
| 2 | STACK_OVERFLOW | Eval or call stack exceeds limit |
| 3 | STACK_UNDERFLOW | Eval stack pop when empty |
| 4 | INVALID_OPCODE | Unknown opcode byte |
| 5 | INVALID_ADDRESS | Memory access out of bounds |
| 6 | NIL_POINTER | Dereference of address 0 |
| 7 | BOUNDS_CHECK | Array index out of bounds |

On trap: VM sets status=2, trap_code=N, halts. Diagnostic message to UART.

## 8. Memory Segment Layout

At VM initialization:

1. **Code segment** starts at a fixed base address (after VM code)
2. **Constants/data** follows code (emitted by pasm)
3. **Globals** follow data (allocated per `.global` directives)
4. **Call stack** starts after globals, grows upward
5. **Eval stack** starts after call stack region, grows upward
6. **Heap** starts after eval stack region, grows upward
7. Segment base addresses stored in VM state struct

Sizes are configurable at VM init time. Default allocations:
- Call stack: 4 KB
- Eval stack: 2 KB
- Heap: remaining SRAM up to ~900 KB

## 9. Implementation Strategy

### Phase 1: Minimal VM
- Opcode dispatch loop
- push, dup, drop, add, sub, halt
- UART output via sys PUTC
- Hardcoded test bytecode (no pasm yet)

### Phase 2: Control Flow + Procedures
- jmp, jz, jnz
- call, ret, enter, leave
- loadl, storel, loada, storea
- Frame management

### Phase 3: Full Instruction Set
- All arithmetic/logic/comparison opcodes
- Globals (loadg, storeg)
- Indirect memory (load, store, loadb, storeb)
- Nonlocal access (loadn, storen)
- All sys calls

### Phase 4: P-Code Assembler
- Lexer for pasm syntax (UART input)
- Two-pass assembly (collect labels, emit bytes)
- Directive handling (.const, .global, .data, .proc/.end)
- Output bytecode to memory buffer

### Phase 5: Integration & Test Suite
- pasm assembles test programs
- VM executes assembled bytecode
- Regression test harness (demo.sh)
- Full test coverage per staged plan

## 10. COR24 Assembly Patterns

Reference patterns from tf24a for common idioms:

### Dispatch Table
```asm
; Index into jump table by opcode byte
; r0 = opcode
la r2, dispatch_table
; multiply r0 by 3 (entry size = .word = 3 bytes)
; ... load target address, jmp
```

### UART Output
```asm
; r0 = character to send
la r2, -65280       ; UART base
tx_wait:
    lb r0, 1(r2)   ; status (sign-extended)
    cls r0, z       ; C = (status < 0) = TX busy
    brt tx_wait
    sb r0, 0(r2)   ; write byte
```

### Memory Struct Access
```asm
; Load vm_state.pc into r0
la fp, vm_state
lw r0, 0(fp)       ; pc at offset 0
```

    77→         ├──────────────────────┤

    79→         ├──────────────────────┤
    80→         │ Call stack            │  frames: ret-pc, dyn-link, static-link,
    81→         │ (grows upward)       │  proc-id, locals
    82→         ├──────────────────────┤
    83→         │ Eval stack            │  expression temporaries
    84→         │ (grows upward)       │  (separate from call stack)
    85→         ├──────────────────────┤
    86→         │ Free space            │
    87→         ├──────────────────────┤
    88→         │ Heap                  │  dynamic allocation (grows upward)
    89→         │ (bump allocator v1)  │
    90→         ├──────────────────────┤
    91→0x0FFFFF │ End of SRAM           │
    92→         ├──────────────────────┤
    93→0xFEEC00 │ EBR (3KB)             │  COR24 hardware stack (sp)
    94→         │                      │  Used as scratch / COR24-level stack
    95→0xFEF7FF └──────────────────────┘
    96→0xFF0100   UART data register
    97→0xFF0101   UART status register
    98→0xFF0200   LED port
    99→```
   100→
   101→## Register Allocation (COR24 → VM)
   102→
   103→The COR24 has very few registers. For the VM interpreter:
   104→
   105→| COR24 Reg | VM Role         | Notes |
   106→|-----------|-----------------|-------|
   107→| r0        | W (work/scratch)| General scratch, opcode dispatch |
   108→| r1        | VM state ptr    | Points to VM state struct in memory |
   109→| r2        | Scratch #2      | Secondary work register |
   110→| sp        | COR24 stack     | Hardware push/pop (EBR), used sparingly |
   111→| fp        | Memory base     | Used for indexed loads/stores |
   112→
   113→VM state (pc, esp, csp, gp, hp, etc.) lives in a memory struct because
   114→COR24 doesn't have enough registers to hold it all.
   115→
   116→## Execution Model
   117→
   118→### Interpreter Loop (fetch-decode-execute)
   119→
   120→```
   121→vm_loop:
   122→    ; fetch opcode byte from p-code[pc]
   123→    ; pc += 1
   124→    ; decode: index into dispatch table
   125→    ; jump to handler
   126→    ; handler executes, modifies VM state
   127→    ; jump back to vm_loop
   128→```
   129→
   130→### Dual Stack Architecture
   131→
   132→- **Eval stack**: expression temporaries, pushed/popped by arithmetic and
   133→  load/store instructions. Grows upward in SRAM.
   134→- **Call stack**: procedure activation records (frames). Grows upward in
   135→  a separate SRAM region. Each frame contains:
   136→  - Return PC (p-code address)
   137→  - Dynamic link (previous frame pointer)
   138→  - Static link (lexically enclosing frame, for nested scopes)
   139→  - Procedure ID (for debugger)
   140→  - Local variable slots
   141→
   142→Separate stacks make debugging much easier and prevent eval stack corruption
   143→from damaging frame data.
   144→
   145→### Opcode Encoding
   146→
   147→P-code instructions are variable-length byte sequences:
   148→
   149→| Format | Bytes | Example |
   150→|--------|-------|---------|
   151→| op     | 1     | `add`, `dup`, `ret` |
   152→| op imm8 | 2   | `push_small 42` |
   153→| op imm24 | 4  | `push 100000`, `jmp label` |
   154→| op u8 u8 | 3  | `loadl 2` (local at offset 2) |
   155→
   156→## I/O Model
   157→
   158→The VM provides two I/O paths:
   159→
   160→1. **sys instructions** — abstract device calls (portable)
   161→   - `sys PUTC` — write byte to output
   162→   - `sys GETC` — read byte from input
   163→   - `sys LED` — write LED state
   164→   - `sys HALT` — stop execution
   165→
   166→2. **Memory-mapped I/O** — direct UART/LED access via load/store
   167→   - Available but discouraged for portable p-code programs
   168→   - Runtime library wraps MMIO behind sys calls
   169→
   170→## Build & Test
   171→
   172→```bash
   173→# Assemble and run VM with a test program
   174→cor24-run --run pvm.s -u "$(cat tests/t01-arith.pasm)" --speed 0 -n 5000000
   175→
   176→# Or: assemble pasm source, then run VM with bytecode
   177→# (exact mechanism TBD — may use UART pipe or preloaded memory)
   178→
   179→# Interactive mode
   180→cor24-run --run pvm.s --terminal --echo
   181→
   182→# Regression test
   183→./demo.sh test
   184→```

   190→| cor24-rs/cor24-bin | COR24 assembler + emulator | ~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs |
   191→| tf24a | Pattern reference (Forth in COR24 asm) | ~/github/sw-cli-tools/tf24a |
   192→| pv24a | **This project** — P-Code VM + pasm | ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a |

│  Layer 1: P-Code VM                 │  ← this project (core)
│  Stack machine interpreter          │
│  Eval stack + call stack + globals  │
│  ~30 opcodes, frame model, traps    │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤

│  COR24 ISA, cor24-run assembler,    │

└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

tool for testing.

## Memory Map (COR24 address space)

```
0x000000 ┌──────────────────────┐
         │ COR24 boot / entry   │  cor24-run entry point
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ P-Code VM code       │  pvm.s assembled COR24 instructions
         │ (interpreter loop,   │
         │  opcode dispatch,    │
         │  runtime services)   │
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ P-Code bytecode      │  loaded/assembled p-code program
         │ (code segment)       │
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ Constants / strings  │  literal pool
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ Globals segment      │  p-code global variables
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ Call stack            │  frames: ret-pc, dyn-link, static-link,
         │ (grows upward)       │  proc-id, locals
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ Eval stack            │  expression temporaries
         │ (grows upward)       │  (separate from call stack)
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ Free space            │
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ Heap                  │  dynamic allocation (grows upward)
         │ (bump allocator v1)  │
         ├──────────────────────┤
0x0FFFFF │ End of SRAM           │
         ├──────────────────────┤
0xFEEC00 │ EBR (3KB)             │  COR24 hardware stack (sp)
         │                      │  Used as scratch / COR24-level stack
0xFEF7FF └──────────────────────┘
0xFF0100   UART data register
0xFF0101   UART status register
0xFF0200   LED port
```

## Register Allocation (COR24 → VM)

The COR24 has very few registers. For the VM interpreter:

| COR24 Reg | VM Role         | Notes |
|-----------|-----------------|-------|
| r0        | W (work/scratch)| General scratch, opcode dispatch |
| r1        | VM state ptr    | Points to VM state struct in memory |
| r2        | Scratch #2      | Secondary work register |
| sp        | COR24 stack     | Hardware push/pop (EBR), used sparingly |
| fp        | Memory base     | Used for indexed loads/stores |

VM state (pc, esp, csp, gp, hp, etc.) lives in a memory struct because
COR24 doesn't have enough registers to hold it all.

## Execution Model

### Interpreter Loop (fetch-decode-execute)

```
vm_loop:
    ; fetch opcode byte from p-code[pc]
    ; pc += 1
    ; decode: index into dispatch table
    ; jump to handler
    ; handler executes, modifies VM state
    ; jump back to vm_loop
```

### Dual Stack Architecture

- **Eval stack**: expression temporaries, pushed/popped by arithmetic and
  load/store instructions. Grows upward in SRAM.
- **Call stack**: procedure activation records (frames). Grows upward in
  a separate SRAM region. Each frame contains:
  - Return PC (p-code address)
  - Dynamic link (previous frame pointer)
  - Static link (lexically enclosing frame, for nested scopes)
  - Procedure ID (for debugger)
  - Local variable slots

Separate stacks make debugging much easier and prevent eval stack corruption
from damaging frame data.

### Opcode Encoding

P-code instructions are variable-length byte sequences:

| Format | Bytes | Example |
|--------|-------|---------|
| op     | 1     | `add`, `dup`, `ret` |
| op imm8 | 2   | `push_small 42` |
| op imm24 | 4  | `push 100000`, `jmp label` |
| op u8 u8 | 3  | `loadl 2` (local at offset 2) |

## I/O Model

The VM provides two I/O paths:

1. **sys instructions** — abstract device calls (portable)
   - `sys PUTC` — write byte to output
   - `sys GETC` — read byte from input
   - `sys LED` — write LED state
   - `sys HALT` — stop execution

2. **Memory-mapped I/O** — direct UART/LED access via load/store
   - Available but discouraged for portable p-code programs
   - Runtime library wraps MMIO behind sys calls

## Build & Test

```bash
# Assemble and run VM with a test program
cor24-run --run pvm.s -u "$(cat tests/t01-arith.pasm)" --speed 0 -n 5000000

# Or: assemble pasm source, then run VM with bytecode
# (exact mechanism TBD — may use UART pipe or preloaded memory)

# Interactive mode
cor24-run --run pvm.s --terminal --echo

# Regression test
./demo.sh test
```

; forth.s — tf24a DTC Forth: Phases 1-4 (bootstrap, threading, dictionary, interpreter)
; COR24 DTC Forth kernel
;
; Register allocation (frozen):
;   r0 = W (work/scratch)
;   r1 = RSP (return stack pointer, grows down from 0x0F0000)
;   r2 = IP (instruction pointer for threaded code)
;   sp = DSP (data stack, hardware push/pop in EBR)
;   fp = limited scratch (only pop/push/add-as-source work)
;
; UART: data at 0xFF0100 (-65280), status at 0xFF0101 (-65279)
;   TX busy = status bit 7, RX ready = status bit 0
;
; DTC NEXT (inlined at tail of every primitive, 5 bytes):
;   lw r0, 0(r2)    ; W = mem[IP] — fetch code address from thread
;   add r2, 3       ; IP += cell
;   jmp (r0)        ; execute code
;
; Colon word CFA formats:
;   Near (hand-assembled, within 127B of do_docol):
;     bra do_docol     ; 2 bytes
;     .byte 0          ; 1 byte pad — PFA at CFA+3
;   Far (runtime-compiled or distant):
;     push r0          ; 1 byte — save CFA on data stack
;     la r0, do_docol_far ; 4 bytes
;     jmp (r0)         ; 1 byte — PFA at CFA+6
;
; Dictionary header layout:
;   .word link    ; 3 bytes — link to previous entry (0 = end)
;   .byte flags   ; 1 byte — bit7=IMMEDIATE, bit6=HIDDEN, bits0-5=namelen
;   .byte c1..cN  ; N bytes — name characters
;   (CFA follows immediately: CFA = entry + 4 + namelen)

; ============================================================
; Entry point (address 0)
; ============================================================
_start:
    la r1, 983040       ; r1 = 0x0F0000 return stack base

    ; Initialize system variables (r0, r2 free before Phase 1)
    la r2, entry_bye
    la r0, var_latest_val
    sw r2, 0(r0)        ; LATEST = last dictionary entry
    la r2, dict_end
    la r0, var_here_val
    sw r2, 0(r0)        ; HERE = first free byte

    ; ============================================================
    ; Phase 1: Inline Tests — print "OK\n*\n"
    ; ============================================================

    ; Test 1: Data stack + UART — print "OK\n"
    lc r0, 10           ; '\n'
    push r0
    lc r0, 75           ; 'K'
    push r0
    lc r0, 79           ; 'O'
    push r0

    la r2, -65280       ; r2 = UART base (IP not needed yet)

    ; Emit 'O'
    pop r0
    push r0
tx1:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt tx1
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)

    ; Emit 'K'
    pop r0
    push r0
tx2:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt tx2
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)

    ; Emit '\n'
    pop r0
    push r0
tx3:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt tx3
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)

    ; Test 2: Return stack — push 42, clear, pop, emit '*'
    lc r0, 42
    add r1, -3
    sw r0, 0(r1)
    lc r0, 0
    lw r0, 0(r1)
    add r1, 3

    push r0
tx4:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt tx4
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)

    ; Emit '\n'
    lc r0, 10
    push r0
tx5:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt tx5
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)

    ; ============================================================
    ; Launch threaded code tests (Phase 2 + Phase 3)
    ; ============================================================
    la r2, test_thread  ; IP = start of test thread
    ; NEXT — bootstrap into threaded execution
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    add r2, 3
    jmp (r0)

; ============================================================
; DOCOL — shared entry for colon definitions
; ============================================================

; Near DOCOL: CFA is "bra do_docol; .byte 0" (3 bytes), r0 = CFA from NEXT
do_docol:
    add r1, -3
    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; push IP to return stack
    mov r2, r0           ; r2 = CFA (from NEXT's jmp)
    add r2, 3            ; r2 = PFA = CFA + 3
    ; NEXT
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    add r2, 3
    jmp (r0)

; Far DOCOL: CFA is "push r0; la r0, do_docol_far; jmp (r0)" (6 bytes)
; CFA address was pushed to data stack by "push r0" in the CFA
do_docol_far:
    add r1, -3
    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; push IP to return stack
    pop r2               ; r2 = CFA (from data stack)
    add r2, 6            ; r2 = PFA = CFA + 6
    ; NEXT
    lw r0, 0(r2)

; pvm.s — P-Code Virtual Machine for COR24
;
; Register allocation:
;   r0 = W (work/scratch, opcode dispatch)
;   r1 = secondary scratch
;   r2 = scratch
;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR, used sparingly)
;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
;
; VM state lives in memory struct (vm_state) because COR24 has only 3 GPRs.
;
; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb), RX ready = bit 0
; LED: port at -65024 (0xFF0200)

; ============================================================
; Entry point
; ============================================================
_start:
    ; Initialize VM state struct
    la fp, vm_state

    ; pc = 0
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 0(fp)

    ; esp = eval_stack base
    la r0, eval_stack
    sw r0, 3(fp)

    ; csp = call_stack base
    la r0, call_stack
    sw r0, 6(fp)

    ; fp_vm = 0 (no frame yet)
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 9(fp)

    ; gp = globals base
    la r0, globals_seg
    sw r0, 12(fp)

    ; hp = heap base
    la r0, heap_seg
    sw r0, 15(fp)

    ; code = code segment base
    la r0, code_seg
    sw r0, 18(fp)

    ; status = 0 (running)
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 21(fp)

    ; trap_code = 0
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 24(fp)

    ; Print boot message
    la r0, msg_boot
    push r0
    la r0, uart_puts
    jmp (r0)
boot_ret:

    ; Enter VM main loop
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; ============================================================
; UART helpers
; ============================================================

; uart_putc — send byte in r0 to UART
; Clobbers: r2
uart_putc:
    push r0
    la r2, -65280
uart_putc_wait:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt uart_putc_wait
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    pop r0
    jmp (r0)

; uart_puts — print null-terminated string
; Address on COR24 stack, return via boot_ret
; Clobbers: r0, r1, r2
uart_puts:
    pop r1
    la r2, -65280
uart_puts_loop:
    lb r0, 0(r1)
    cls r0, z
    brf uart_puts_done
    ; Check if byte is zero (lb sign-extends, so zero means zero)
    ; Actually cls z tests sign bit. We need to check if r0 == 0.
    ; lb sign-extends, but if byte is 0, r0 is 0.
    ; We need a different test. Push and use sub.
    ; Let's restructure: load byte, check if zero, if not send it.
    ; Problem: cls only tests sign. We need r0 == 0 test.
    ; Approach: sub 1, check if negative (was 0 or less)
    ; But we'd need to preserve original. Let's use a different pattern.
    bra uart_puts_send
uart_puts_done:
    la r0, boot_ret
    jmp (r0)
uart_puts_send:
    ; r0 has the character (might be negative if >= 128, but still valid)
    push r0
uart_puts_tx:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt uart_puts_tx
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    add r1, 1
    bra uart_puts_loop

; ============================================================
; VM fetch-decode-execute loop
; ============================================================
vm_loop:
    ; Check status: if not running (0), stop
    la fp, vm_state
    lw r0, 21(fp)
    ; If status != 0, halt the COR24
    ; Test: if r0 > 0, halt. cls z tests sign bit (< 0).
    ; For status > 0: subtract 1, if result >= 0 then status >= 1
    push r0
    add r0, -1
    cls r0, z
    pop r0
    brf vm_halted

    ; Fetch opcode byte from code[pc]
    lw r0, 18(fp)
    lw r1, 0(fp)
    add r0, r1
    ; r0 = code_base + pc, use as base for byte load
    ; Need to put address in a base register
    ; Use fp temporarily, save/restore vm_state pointer
    push r0
    pop fp
    lb r0, 0(fp)
    ; r0 = opcode byte (sign-extended, but 0-96 are positive)
    ; Restore fp to vm_state
    la fp, vm_state

    ; Increment pc
    lw r1, 0(fp)
    add r1, 1
    sw r1, 0(fp)

    ; Dispatch: compute dispatch_table[opcode * 3]
    mov r2, r0
    add r0, r0
    add r0, r2
    ; r0 = 3 * opcode
    la r2, dispatch_table
    add r2, r0
    ; r2 = &dispatch_table[opcode * 3]
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = handler address
    jmp (r0)

vm_halted:
    ; Print status message
    ; Check if trapped (status == 2)
    add r0, -2
    cls r0, z
    brf vm_halt_clean
    ; Trapped: print trap message
    la r0, msg_trap
    push r0
    la r0, uart_puts_halt
    jmp (r0)
vm_halt_clean:
    la r0, msg_halted
    push r0
    la r0, uart_puts_halt
    jmp (r0)

; uart_puts_halt — like uart_puts but jumps to halt_loop when done
uart_puts_halt:
    pop r1
    la r2, -65280
uart_puts_halt_loop:
    lb r0, 0(r1)
    cls r0, z
    brf uart_puts_halt_done
    bra uart_puts_halt_send
uart_puts_halt_done:
    bra halt_loop
uart_puts_halt_send:
    push r0
uart_puts_halt_tx:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt uart_puts_halt_tx
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    add r1, 1
    bra uart_puts_halt_loop

halt_loop:
    bra halt_loop

; ============================================================
; Opcode handlers (all stubs for now)
; ============================================================

; 0x00 — halt
op_halt:
    la fp, vm_state
    lc r0, 1
    sw r0, 21(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

    sw r0, 21(fp)
    lc r0, 4
    sw r0, 24(fp)

    jmp (r0)

    ; 0x26-0x2F: reserved (gap)

ISA Reference
COR24 Instruction Set Reference
C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit. 32 opcodes, 211 instruction forms (1, 2, or 4 bytes). See makerlisp.com for the hardware specification.

CPU State
All registers and addresses are 24 bits wide (values 0 to 16,777,215).

State	Description
PC	Program counter — address of next instruction. Starts at 0.
C	Condition flag — single bit, set by compare instructions (ceq, clu, cls), tested by branches (brt, brf). Also writable via mov ra, c and clu z, ra.
Registers
Register	Name	Width	Purpose
r0		24-bit	General purpose
r1		24-bit	General purpose / return address (jal convention)
r2		24-bit	General purpose
fp		24-bit	Frame pointer — base for stack-frame locals
sp		24-bit	Stack pointer — init 0xFEEC00, grows downward (3 bytes per push)
z		24-bit	Hardwired to zero. Readable in compares: ceq r0, z
iv		24-bit	Interrupt vector — address of interrupt service routine
ir		24-bit	Interrupt return — saved PC when interrupt fires. Return with jmp (ir)
Only r0, r1, r2 can be destinations for most ALU/load instructions. fp can be pushed/popped and used as a memory base register. sp is modified by push, pop, and sub sp.

Load Constants
Instruction	Bytes	Description
lc ra, dd	2	Load signed 8-bit constant (-128..127). Sign-extends to 24 bits.
lcu ra, dd	2	Load unsigned 8-bit constant (0..255). Zero-extends to 24 bits.
la ra, addr	4	Load 24-bit address/constant. Any value 0..16777215.
Arithmetic
Instruction	Bytes	Description
add ra, rb	1	ra = ra + rb
add ra, dd	2	ra = ra + dd (signed 8-bit immediate)
sub ra, rb	1	ra = ra - rb
sub sp, addr	4	sp = sp - addr (24-bit; allocate stack space)
mul ra, rb	1	ra = ra * rb (24-bit result, overflow wraps)
Logic & Shifts
Instruction	Bytes	Description
and ra, rb	1	ra = ra AND rb
or ra, rb	1	ra = ra OR rb
xor ra, rb	1	ra = ra XOR rb
shl ra, rb	1	ra = ra << rb (shift left)
srl ra, rb	1	ra = ra >> rb (shift right, zero fill)
sra ra, rb	1	ra = ra >> rb (shift right, sign fill)
Compare (set C flag)
Instruction	Bytes	Description
ceq ra, rb	1	C = (ra == rb)
clu ra, rb	1	C = (ra < rb) unsigned
cls ra, rb	1	C = (ra < rb) signed
Use z register for zero tests: ceq r0, z sets C if r0 == 0.

Branch (PC-relative, signed 8-bit offset)
Instruction	Bytes	Description
bra label	2	Branch always
brt label	2	Branch if C = true
brf label	2	Branch if C = false

content.text:
Now I have the complete ISA reference. I see several issues in my initial pvm.s:
1. Need `lbu` not `lb` for opcode byte fetch (zero-extend vs sign-extend)
2. Need `ceq r0, z` for zero tests, not `cls r0, z` (which tests sign)
3. Use `jal r1, (r2)` for function calls
4. Valid base registers: r0, r1, r2, fp (not sp)

Let me rewrite pvm.s properly.

content.content:
1→; pvm.s — P-Code Virtual Machine for COR24
     2→;
     3→; Register allocation:
     4→;   r0 = W (work/scratch, opcode dispatch)
     5→;   r1 = secondary scratch
     6→;   r2 = scratch
     7→;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR, used sparingly)
     8→;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
     9→;
    10→; VM state lives in memory struct (vm_state) because COR24 has only 3 GPRs.
    11→;
    12→; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
    13→;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb), RX ready = bit 0
    14→; LED: port at -65024 (0xFF0200)
    15→
    16→; ============================================================
    17→; Entry point
    18→; ============================================================
    19→_start:
    20→    ; Initialize VM state struct
    21→    la fp, vm_state
    22→
    23→    ; pc = 0
    24→    lc r0, 0
    25→    sw r0, 0(fp)
    26→
    27→    ; esp = eval_stack base
    28→    la r0, eval_stack
    29→    sw r0, 3(fp)
    30→
    31→    ; csp = call_stack base
    32→    la r0, call_stack
    33→    sw r0, 6(fp)
    34→
    35→    ; fp_vm = 0 (no frame yet)
    36→    lc r0, 0
    37→    sw r0, 9(fp)
    38→
    39→    ; gp = globals base
    40→    la r0, globals_seg
    41→    sw r0, 12(fp)
    42→
    43→    ; hp = heap base
    44→    la r0, heap_seg
    45→    sw r0, 15(fp)
    46→
    47→    ; code = code segment base
    48→    la r0, code_seg
    49→    sw r0, 18(fp)
    50→
    51→    ; status = 0 (running)
    52→    lc r0, 0
    53→    sw r0, 21(fp)
    54→
    55→    ; trap_code = 0
    56→    lc r0, 0
    57→    sw r0, 24(fp)
    58→
    59→    ; Print boot message
    60→    la r0, msg_boot
    61→    push r0
    62→    la r0, uart_puts
    63→    jmp (r0)
    64→boot_ret:
    65→
    66→    ; Enter VM main loop
    67→    la r0, vm_loop
    68→    jmp (r0)
    69→
    70→; ============================================================
    71→; UART helpers
    72→; ============================================================
    73→
    74→; uart_putc — send byte in r0 to UART
    75→; Clobbers: r2
    76→uart_putc:
    77→    push r0
    78→    la r2, -65280
    79→uart_putc_wait:
    80→    lb r0, 1(r2)
    81→    cls r0, z
    82→    brt uart_putc_wait
    83→    pop r0
    84→    sb r0, 0(r2)
    85→    pop r0
    86→    jmp (r0)
    87→
    88→; uart_puts — print null-terminated string
    89→; Address on COR24 stack, return via boot_ret
    90→; Clobbers: r0, r1, r2
    91→uart_puts:
    92→    pop r1
    93→    la r2, -65280
    94→uart_puts_loop:
    95→    lb r0, 0(r1)
    96→    cls r0, z
    97→    brf uart_puts_done
    98→    ; Check if byte is zero (lb sign-extends, so zero means zero)
    99→    ; Actually cls z tests sign bit. We need to check if r0 == 0.
   100→    ; lb sign-extends, but if byte is 0, r0 is 0.
   101→    ; We need a different test. Push and use sub.
   102→    ; Let's restructure: load byte, check if zero, if not send it.
   103→    ; Problem: cls only tests sign. We need r0 == 0 test.
   104→    ; Approach: sub 1, check if negative (was 0 or less)
   105→    ; But we'd need to preserve original. Let's use a different pattern.
   106→    bra uart_puts_send
   107→uart_puts_done:
   108→    la r0, boot_ret
   109→    jmp (r0)
   110→uart_puts_send:
   111→    ; r0 has the character (might be negative if >= 128, but still valid)
   112→    push r0
   113→uart_puts_tx:
   114→    lb r0, 1(r2)
   115→    cls r0, z
   116→    brt uart_puts_tx
   117→    pop r0
   118→    sb r0, 0(r2)
   119→    add r1, 1
   120→    bra uart_puts_loop
   121→
   122→; ============================================================
   123→; VM fetch-decode-execute loop
   124→; ============================================================
   125→vm_loop:
   126→    ; Check status: if not running (0), stop
   127→    la fp, vm_state
   128→    lw r0, 21(fp)
   129→    ; If status != 0, halt the COR24
   130→    ; Test: if r0 > 0, halt. cls z tests sign bit (< 0).
   131→    ; For status > 0: subtract 1, if result >= 0 then status >= 1
   132→    push r0
   133→    add r0, -1
   134→    cls r0, z
   135→    pop r0
   136→    brf vm_halted
   137→
   138→    ; Fetch opcode byte from code[pc]
   139→    lw r0, 18(fp)
   140→    lw r1, 0(fp)
   141→    add r0, r1
   142→    ; r0 = code_base + pc, use as base for byte load
   143→    ; Need to put address in a base register
   144→    ; Use fp temporarily, save/restore vm_state pointer

file.content:
; pvm.s — P-Code Virtual Machine for COR24
;
; Register allocation:
;   r0 = W (work/scratch, opcode dispatch)
;   r1 = secondary scratch
;   r2 = scratch
;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR, used sparingly)
;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
;
; VM state lives in memory struct (vm_state) because COR24 has only 3 GPRs.
;
; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb), RX ready = bit 0
; LED: port at -65024 (0xFF0200)

; ============================================================
; Entry point
; ============================================================
_start:
    ; Initialize VM state struct
    la fp, vm_state

    ; pc = 0
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 0(fp)

    ; esp = eval_stack base
    la r0, eval_stack
    sw r0, 3(fp)

    ; csp = call_stack base
    la r0, call_stack
    sw r0, 6(fp)

    ; fp_vm = 0 (no frame yet)
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 9(fp)

    ; gp = globals base
    la r0, globals_seg
    sw r0, 12(fp)

    ; hp = heap base
    la r0, heap_seg
    sw r0, 15(fp)

    ; code = code segment base
    la r0, code_seg
    sw r0, 18(fp)

    ; status = 0 (running)
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 21(fp)

    ; trap_code = 0
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 24(fp)

    ; Print boot message
    la r0, msg_boot
    push r0
    la r0, uart_puts
    jmp (r0)
boot_ret:

    ; Enter VM main loop
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; ============================================================
; UART helpers
; ============================================================

; uart_putc — send byte in r0 to UART
; Clobbers: r2
uart_putc:
    push r0
    la r2, -65280
uart_putc_wait:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt uart_putc_wait
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    pop r0
    jmp (r0)

; uart_puts — print null-terminated string
; Address on COR24 stack, return via boot_ret
; Clobbers: r0, r1, r2
uart_puts:
    pop r1
    la r2, -65280
uart_puts_loop:
    lb r0, 0(r1)
    cls r0, z
    brf uart_puts_done
    ; Check if byte is zero (lb sign-extends, so zero means zero)
    ; Actually cls z tests sign bit. We need to check if r0 == 0.
    ; lb sign-extends, but if byte is 0, r0 is 0.
    ; We need a different test. Push and use sub.
    ; Let's restructure: load byte, check if zero, if not send it.
    ; Problem: cls only tests sign. We need r0 == 0 test.
    ; Approach: sub 1, check if negative (was 0 or less)
    ; But we'd need to preserve original. Let's use a different pattern.
    bra uart_puts_send
uart_puts_done:
    la r0, boot_ret
    jmp (r0)
uart_puts_send:
    ; r0 has the character (might be negative if >= 128, but still valid)
    push r0
uart_puts_tx:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt uart_puts_tx
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    add r1, 1
    bra uart_puts_loop

; ============================================================
; VM fetch-decode-execute loop
; ============================================================
vm_loop:
    ; Check status: if not running (0), stop
    la fp, vm_state
    lw r0, 21(fp)
    ; If status != 0, halt the COR24
    ; Test: if r0 > 0, halt. cls z tests sign bit (< 0).
    ; For status > 0: subtract 1, if result >= 0 then status >= 1
    push r0
    add r0, -1
    cls r0, z
    pop r0
    brf vm_halted

    ; Fetch opcode byte from code[pc]
    lw r0, 18(fp)
    lw r1, 0(fp)
    add r0, r1
    ; r0 = code_base + pc, use as base for byte load
    ; Need to put address in a base register
    ; Use fp temporarily, save/restore vm_state pointer
    push r0
    pop fp
    lb r0, 0(fp)
    ; r0 = opcode byte (sign-extended, but 0-96 are positive)
    ; Restore fp to vm_state
    la fp, vm_state

    ; Increment pc
    lw r1, 0(fp)
    add r1, 1
    sw r1, 0(fp)

    ; Dispatch: compute dispatch_table[opcode * 3]
    mov r2, r0
    add r0, r0
    add r0, r2
    ; r0 = 3 * opcode
    la r2, dispatch_table
    add r2, r0
    ; r2 = &dispatch_table[opcode * 3]
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = handler address
    jmp (r0)

vm_halted:
    ; Print status message
    ; Check if trapped (status == 2)
    add r0, -2
    cls r0, z
    brf vm_halt_clean
    ; Trapped: print trap message
    la r0, msg_trap
    push r0
    la r0, uart_puts_halt
    jmp (r0)
vm_halt_clean:
    la r0, msg_halted
    push r0
    la r0, uart_puts_halt
    jmp (r0)

; uart_puts_halt — like uart_puts but jumps to halt_loop when done
uart_puts_halt:
    pop r1
    la r2, -65280
uart_puts_halt_loop:
    lb r0, 0(r1)
    cls r0, z
    brf uart_puts_halt_done
    bra uart_puts_halt_send
uart_puts_halt_done:
    bra halt_loop
uart_puts_halt_send:
    push r0
uart_puts_halt_tx:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt uart_puts_halt_tx
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    add r1, 1
    bra uart_puts_halt_loop

halt_loop:
    bra halt_loop

; ============================================================
; Opcode handlers (all stubs for now)
; ============================================================

; 0x00 — halt
op_halt:
    la fp, vm_state
    lc r0, 1
    sw r0, 21(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; Invalid opcode handler
op_invalid:
    la fp, vm_state
    lc r0, 2
    sw r0, 21(fp)
    lc r0, 4
    sw r0, 24(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; Stub handler — jumps to op_invalid (used for all unimplemented opcodes)
op_stub:
    la r0, op_invalid
    jmp (r0)

; ============================================================
; Dispatch table (97 entries: opcodes 0x00 through 0x60)
; Each entry is a .word (3 bytes) holding the handler address
; ============================================================
dispatch_table:
    ; 0x00-0x06: Stack / Constants
    .word op_halt
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    ; 0x07-0x0F: reserved (gap)
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    ; 0x10-0x1B: Arithmetic / Logic
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    ; 0x1C-0x1F: reserved (gap)
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    ; 0x20-0x25: Comparison
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    ; 0x26-0x2F: reserved (gap)
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    ; 0x30-0x35: Control Flow
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    ; 0x36-0x3F: reserved (gap)
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    ; 0x40-0x4B: Local / Global / Nonlocal Access
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    ; 0x4C-0x4F: reserved (gap)
    .word op_invalid

    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    ; 0x50-0x53: Indirect Memory Access
    .word op_stub

    .word op_stub
    ; 0x54-0x5F: reserved (gap)

    ; 0x60: sys

; ============================================================
; String constants
; ============================================================
msg_boot:
    .byte 80, 86, 77, 32, 79, 75, 10, 0
    ; "PVM OK\n\0"

msg_halted:

    ; "HALT\n\0"

msg_trap:

    ; "TRAP\n\0"

; ============================================================

; ============================================================

    .word 0
    ; pc (offset 0)

; pvm.s — P-Code Virtual Machine for COR24
;
; Register allocation:
;   r0 = W (work/scratch, opcode dispatch)
;   r1 = scratch / return address for jal calls
;   r2 = scratch
;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR, used sparingly)
;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
;
; VM state lives in memory struct (vm_state) because COR24 has only 3 GPRs.
;
; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb sign-extend)
;   RX ready = status bit 0
; LED: port at -65024 (0xFF0200)
;
; COR24 ISA notes:
;   lbu = load byte zero-extend, lb = load byte sign-extend
;   ceq ra, rb sets C if ra == rb; cls ra, rb sets C if ra < rb (signed)
;   clu ra, rb sets C if ra < rb (unsigned)
;   brt/brf = branch if C true/false; bra = branch always
;   jal r1, (r2) = r1 = PC+1, PC = r2 (call convention)
;   jmp (r1) = return from jal call
;   Valid load/store base registers: r0, r1, r2, fp (NOT sp)
;   Can push/pop: r0, r1, r2, fp

; ============================================================
; Entry point
; ============================================================
_start:
    ; Initialize VM state struct
    la fp, vm_state

    ; pc = 0
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 0(fp)

    ; esp = eval_stack base
    la r0, eval_stack
    sw r0, 3(fp)

    ; csp = call_stack base
    la r0, call_stack
    sw r0, 6(fp)

    ; fp_vm = 0 (no frame yet)
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 9(fp)

    ; gp = globals base
    la r0, globals_seg
    sw r0, 12(fp)

    ; hp = heap base
    la r0, heap_seg
    sw r0, 15(fp)

    ; code = code segment base
    la r0, code_seg
    sw r0, 18(fp)

    ; status = 0 (running)
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 21(fp)

    ; trap_code = 0
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 24(fp)

    ; Print boot message
    la r0, msg_boot
    la r2, uart_puts
    jal r1, (r2)

    ; Enter VM main loop
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; ============================================================
; UART helpers
; ============================================================

; uart_putc — send byte in r0 to UART
; Call via: jal r1, (r2) with r2 = uart_putc
; Clobbers: r0, r2 (r1 = return address, preserved)
uart_putc:
    push r0
    la r2, -65280
uart_putc_wait:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt uart_putc_wait
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    jmp (r1)

; uart_puts — print null-terminated string at address in r0
; Call via: jal r1, (r2) with r2 = uart_puts, r0 = string addr
; Clobbers: r0, r1, r2
uart_puts:
    push r1
    mov r1, r0
uart_puts_loop:
    lbu r0, 0(r1)
    ceq r0, z
    brt uart_puts_done
    push r1
    push r0
    la r2, -65280
uart_puts_tx:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt uart_puts_tx
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    pop r1
    add r1, 1
    bra uart_puts_loop
uart_puts_done:
    pop r1
    jmp (r1)

; ============================================================
; VM fetch-decode-execute loop
; ============================================================
vm_loop:

    lc r0, 0

    la r0, eval_stack
    sw r0, 3(fp)

    la r0, call_stack

    ; status = 0 (running)
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 21(fp)

    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 24(fp)

    ; Print boot message
    la r0, msg_boot
    push r0
    la r0, uart_puts
    jmp (r0)

    ; Enter VM main loop
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; ============================================================
; UART helpers
; ============================================================

; uart_putc — send byte in r0 to UART

uart_putc:
    push r0

uart_putc_wait:

    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    pop r0

    add r0, r0
    add r0, r2
    ; r0 = 3 * opcode
    la r2, dispatch_table
    add r2, r0
    ; r2 = &dispatch_table[opcode * 3]
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = handler address
    jmp (r0)

; ============================================================
; VM halt / trap exit
; ============================================================
vm_halted:
    ; r0 = status (nonzero)
    ; Check if trapped (status == 2)
    lc r2, 2
    ceq r0, r2
    brt vm_trapped
    ; Normal halt
    la r0, msg_halted
    la r2, uart_puts_final
    jal r1, (r2)
vm_trapped:
    la r0, msg_trap
    la r2, uart_puts_final
    jal r1, (r2)

; uart_puts_final — print string then enter halt loop
; r0 = string address. Does not return.
uart_puts_final:
    mov r1, r0
uart_puts_final_loop:
    lbu r0, 0(r1)
    ceq r0, z
    brt halt_loop
    push r1
    push r0
    la r2, -65280
uart_puts_final_tx:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt uart_puts_final_tx
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    pop r1
    add r1, 1
    bra uart_puts_final_loop

halt_loop:
    bra halt_loop

; ============================================================
; Opcode handlers (all stubs for now)
; ============================================================

; 0x00 — halt: set status=1, return to vm_loop
op_halt:
    la r0, vm_state
    push r0
    pop fp
    lc r0, 1
    sw r0, 21(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; Invalid opcode: set status=2, trap_code=4 (INVALID_OPCODE)
op_invalid:
    la fp, vm_state
    lc r0, 2
    sw r0, 21(fp)
    lc r0, 4
    sw r0, 24(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; Stub handler — all unimplemented opcodes trap as invalid
op_stub:
    la r0, op_invalid
    jmp (r0)

; ============================================================
; Dispatch table (97 entries: opcodes 0x00 through 0x60)
; Each entry is a .word (3 bytes) holding the handler address
; ============================================================
dispatch_table:
    ; 0x00-0x06: Stack / Constants
    .word op_halt
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    ; 0x07-0x0F: reserved (gap)
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    ; 0x10-0x1B: Arithmetic / Logic
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    ; 0x1C-0x1F: reserved (gap)
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    ; 0x20-0x25: Comparison
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    ; 0x26-0x2F: reserved (gap)
    .word op_invalid

    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid

    ; 0x30-0x35: Control Flow
    .word op_stub

    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    ; 0x36-0x3F: reserved (gap)
    .word op_invalid

    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    .word op_invalid
    ; 0x40-0x4B: Local / Global / Nonlocal Access
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub
    .word op_stub

    ; 0x50-0x53: Indirect Memory Access
    .word op_stub

; ============================================================
; String constants
; ============================================================
msg_boot:
    .byte 80, 86, 77, 32, 79, 75, 10, 0

msg_halted:
